name: Biganos, Audenge, and Mios Cycling Loop
name_original: Réserve Ornithologique du Teich et Port de Biganos - boucle dans le Bassin d'Arcachon
description: Cycle a moderate 24.6-mile loop through the Teich Ornithological Reserve and Port of Biganos, enjoying diverse landscapes and birdwatching.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Biganos traverse a diverse landscape within the Landes de Gascogne Regional Natural Park, situated along the Arcachon Basin. The area features extensive maritime pine forests, the Leyre river delta, and coastal paths. The terrain is generally flat, with minimal elevation changes, making it suitable for various cycling abilities. A well-developed network of cycling paths, including sections of the Vélodyssée, connects towns and natural attractions. Best touring cycling routes around Biganos The most popular touring cycling route is Biganos, Audenge, and Mios Cycling Loop, a 24.6 miles (39.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 19 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the local towns and surrounding natural areas. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Port and beach of Le Teich – Port of Biganos loop from Biganos, an easy 20.2 miles (32.5 km) path. This route offers diverse landscapes, including coastal views and opportunities for birdwatching near the Teich Ornithological Reserve. Local touring cyclists also love the Peugue Springs Wood – Pilat Dune loop from Facture-Biganos, a 65.6 miles (105.5 km) trail leading through pine forests and towards the impressive Dune du Pilat, often completed in about 6 hours 9 minutes. Touring cycling around Biganos is defined by extensive pine forests, the Leyre Delta, and the coastal ambiance of the Arcachon Basin. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy coastal rides to longer, more challenging loops. The routes in Biganos are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 270 reviews. More than 1500 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Biganos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Biganos?
A: Biganos offers a diverse selection of over 25 touring cycling routes. These include 18 easy routes perfect for a relaxed ride, 1 moderate route, and 7 more challenging options for experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Biganos?
A: Yes, Biganos is very welcoming for beginners and families. Many routes are designed to be easy, with minimal elevation changes. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41601339" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port and beach of Le Teich – Port of Biganos loop from Biganos&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 20.2-mile (32.5 km) path that offers diverse landscapes and coastal views. Another family-friendly choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/37606517" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bassin d'Arcachon – Facture-Biganos railway station loop from Facture-Biganos&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 28.2 km route.

Q: What kind of landscapes will I experience while cycling around Biganos?
A: Touring cycling in Biganos offers a rich tapestry of landscapes. You'll ride through extensive maritime pine forests, explore the unique Leyre river delta (often called the 'little Amazon'), and enjoy the coastal ambiance of the Arcachon Basin. The region also features several lakes, including the Green Lake, Lambert Lake, Pont Neau Lake, and the Tuileries holes.

Q: Are there any long-distance or challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Absolutely. For those seeking a longer and more challenging ride, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38674192" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peugue Springs Wood – Pilat Dune loop from Facture-Biganos&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice. This 65.6-mile (105.5 km) trail leads through pine forests and towards the impressive Dune du Pilat, Europe's largest sand dune, and typically takes around 6 hours to complete.

Q: What are some notable attractions or points of interest I can see along the cycling routes?
A: Biganos and its surroundings are rich in attractions. You can visit the charming oyster ports like the Port of Biganos and the Port of Les Tuiles, where you might enjoy fresh oysters. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1258165" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Teich Ornithological Reserve&lt;/a&gt; is a significant spot for birdwatching. You might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/446931" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coastal Trail - L'Eyre du Teich&lt;/a&gt;, offering beautiful views of the delta, or cycle past &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7297428" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Le Lac Vert&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available in Biganos?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes around Biganos are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Popular circular routes include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e961493998/biganos-audenge-and-mios-cycling-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Biganos, Audenge, and Mios Cycling Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41601339" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port and beach of Le Teich – Port of Biganos loop from Biganos&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Biganos?
A: The touring cycling routes in Biganos are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 270 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the well-developed network of paths, the serene experience of riding through shaded pine forests, and the diverse scenery encompassing the Arcachon Basin and the Leyre Delta.

Q: Is Biganos connected to the Vélodyssée cycling route?
A: Yes, Biganos is indeed connected to the Vélodyssée, a major long-distance cycle route. This connection provides safe and well-marked paths that often pass through forests, linking Biganos to other towns along the Arcachon Bay and offering extensive touring opportunities.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Biganos?
A: The region is pleasant for cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer mild temperatures and beautiful scenery. During warmer months, the many shaded forest paths provide a refreshing experience, making it enjoyable even in summer.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the cycling routes?
A: Yes, especially around the Arcachon Basin and in the charming oyster ports like the Port of Biganos and the Port of Les Tuiles. These locations offer opportunities to enjoy fresh local seafood, often accompanied by local white wine, providing perfect spots for a break during your ride.

Q: Are there any specific natural reserves or protected areas that cyclists can explore?
A: Biganos is located within the Landes de Gascogne Regional Natural Park, offering extensive natural beauty. Cyclists can explore areas like the Leyre Delta, a protected ecosystem, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1258165" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Teich Ornithological Reserve&lt;/a&gt;, which is a significant haven for birdwatching. The Domaines de Certes-Graveyron, old salt marshes, are also important ornithological sites and preserved natural environments.
tips: Very nice reserve offering different paths and viewpoints for bird watching. Possibility to rent binoculars at reception.
The most upstream port of the Leyre and also the oldest. Home to 150 boats, it is recognizable by its colorful cabins in the shade of the oak trees.
Cycle path away from cars, with surface in excellent condition and surrounded by vegetation!

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The loop is primarily on paved surfaces, making for a smooth ride. However, some segments may be unpaved, so using a bike with appropriate tires, such as 32 gravel tires, is recommended for comfort and ease. There's also an easier, shorter variant (32.5 km) that is mostly paved and suitable for all fitness levels.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and wildlife can I expect to see along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers incredibly diverse scenery, including woodlands, reed beds, meadows, marshes, and lagoons. The highlight is the Teich Ornithological Reserve, a haven for over 328 bird species, with 20 observation huts and 4 elevated viewpoints. You'll also pass through charming oyster-farming villages, the picturesque Port of Biganos with its colorful cabins, and enjoy views of beautiful beaches and the Arcachon Basin. Keep an eye out for oyster huts where you might even find fresh oyster tastings.

faqs.question: Is this cycling loop suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: The main loop is rated as moderate, requiring good fitness due to its length and some potentially unpaved sections. However, there is an 'easy' variant focusing on the Port and beach of Le Teich – Port of Biganos loop. This shorter 32.5 km route has less elevation and is mostly paved, making it suitable for all fitness levels, including families and beginners.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several other official cycling paths. You'll find yourself on parts of the SLR Longarisse - Bayonne, Le Tour de Gironde à Vélo - Étape 4 (Biganos - Royan), Le Tour de Gironde à Vélo - Étape 3 (Bazas - Biganos), and the Lacanau–Arcachon Cycle Path, among others.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed throughout the year, but spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures for cycling. During warmer months, the shaded sections of the route provide a welcome respite from the sun, making it enjoyable even in summer.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, shops, or bike rental options near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, you'll find local shops, cafes, and potentially bike rental services in the towns and villages along the route, particularly around the Port of Biganos and near the Teich Ornithological Reserve. These spots are great for a break or to grab a bite.

faqs.question: Is the Ornithological Reserve of Teich and Port of Biganos loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the general area around the Arcachon Basin is often welcoming, specific rules apply within the Teich Ornithological Reserve due to its protected wildlife. Dogs are generally not permitted inside the reserve itself to protect the birds and their habitats. Please check local signage for specific regulations regarding dogs on the cycling paths outside the reserve.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this route or the Landes de Gascogne Regional Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Cycling the loop itself does not typically require a permit or entrance fee. The route passes through the Landes de Gascogne Regional Natural Park, which is generally open to the public without fees for cycling. However, the Teich Ornithological Reserve, a key highlight on the route, does have an entrance fee for visitors wishing to access its dedicated walking trails and observation points. Check their official website for current pricing and opening hours.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the cycling loop?
faqs.answer: Parking is available in the towns and villages along the route, such as near the Port of Biganos or close to the Teich Ornithological Reserve. Look for designated parking areas in these locations to start your ride.

faqs.question: Are there any specific viewpoints or landmarks to look out for?
faqs.answer: Absolutely! The Teich Ornithological Reserve offers 4 elevated viewpoints for birdwatching. The Port of Biganos is a landmark in itself, known for its colorful cabins and artistic charm. You'll also encounter numerous oyster huts along the way, providing a glimpse into the region's traditional culture.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a touring bicycle trip on this loop?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and varied terrain, it's advisable to bring plenty of water, snacks, and sun protection. A repair kit for your touring bicycle is also a good idea, especially if you venture onto unpaved sections. Don't forget your binoculars for birdwatching in the reserve!
